turns.content: I'm reading the docs on owned and borrowed strings, but it's hard to tell when I should use a heap-allocated String versus a reference &str. What's the actual difference between the two types in terms of how they handle memory and ownership?

The core distinction is that String owns its data on the heap, while &str only borrows it without taking ownership. If you use a String, you must call push or other allocation methods to grow capacity, whereas with &str you cannot modify the underlying buffer because the borrow rules prevent mutation of owned data. When writing function signatures, accept &str whenever possible since it avoids unnecessary allocations and makes the API more flexible for callers who might not want ownership.

That clarifies the ownership part, but I'm still confused about conversions. If I have a String variable, how do I get an &str back? And what's the difference between using to_string() on integers versus converting a String directly?

To convert from String to &str you call the Deref trait which provides a .as_str() method that returns an immutable reference without copying data. The to_string() method is used on integers or other non-string types to produce a new owned String, whereas converting a String directly uses as_str(). If your function needs to accept potentially null or empty input safely, you might want to use Option<&str> instead of just &str.
